name: Kenny G
abstract: Kenneth Bruce Gorelick (born June 5, 1956) is an American smooth jazz saxophonist, composer, and record producer. His 1986 album Duotones brought him commercial success.

about_quotes.speaker: Ray Drummond
about_quotes.evidence: I have to say somebody that’s almost a perfect example of somebody who has his own sound and obviously is a wonderful saxophone player but it ain’t telling us nothing is Kenny G as a good example.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ray Drummond interviewed by Monk Rowe, Toronto, Canada, January 9, 2003

about_quotes.speaker: Lou Donaldson
about_quotes.evidence: I bet him $100 to $500 that I could stand down at the Apollo Theater and pool the people when they came by, and it would be ten times more people that knew Kenny G than knew Sonny Rollins.
about_quotes.doc_title: Lou Donaldson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Larry Ridley
about_quotes.evidence: Case in point there’s a thing in the new Jazz Times where Gary Giddins is talking about Kenny G... Kenny G supposedly attributes the fact that Charlie Parker got his nickname Bird...
about_quotes.doc_title: Larry Ridley interviewed by Monk Rowe, Toronto, Canada, January 10, 2003

about_quotes.speaker: Genevieve Rose
about_quotes.evidence: Did you ever get into what, for lack of a better term nowadays is called contemporary jazz, the Kenny G type of thing? ... No. I wasn’t really involved with that.
about_quotes.doc_title: Genevieve Rose interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, March 14, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Jason Marsalis
about_quotes.evidence: That’s what I thought it was. I thought it was a lot of – like some of the more ’80s, more watered down, more mainstream music. That’s what I thought it was.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jason Marsalis — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Bob Wilber
about_quotes.evidence: I mean I listened to Kenny G, how can you help but listen to Kenny G. Every time you pick up a phone and you’re put on hold, there’s Kenny G.
about_quotes.doc_title: Bob Wilber interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, May 22, 1998
